title: Jambalaya
subtitle: Louisiana one-pot rice with chicken, sausage, and the holy trinity
excerpt: The Louisiana one-pot: chicken thighs, smoked sausage, and long-grain rice cooked together in a Creole spice base of onion, celery, pepper, garlic, and tinned tomatoes. The rice absorbs the stock and all the flavour from the meat and vegetables. Everything in one pot, one hour, one of the most satisfying meals in the Southern repertoire.
sourceNotes: Jambalaya is a Louisiana Creole and Cajun dish with roots in Spanish, French, and West African culinary traditions. The name is thought to derive from the French 'jambon' (ham) and the West African 'ya' (rice). It appears in Louisiana cooking throughout the nineteenth century and is one of the most documented dishes of the American South. The distinction between Creole jambalaya (with tomatoes) and Cajun jambalaya (without) reflects the different regional traditions of New Orleans and the rural Cajun country.

content.text: The 'holy trinity' of Cajun and Creole cooking is onion, celery, and green pepper. It functions the way mirepoix does in French cooking, as the aromatic base on which almost every dish in the tradition is built. Cook it properly (at least 8 minutes, until soft and slightly golden) before anything else goes in. Everything else builds on that foundation.

Creole jambalaya includes tomatoes; Cajun jambalaya traditionally does not. This is the Creole version, which is the more widely known internationally. The rice goes in dry and cooks in the stock, absorbing the liquid and all the flavour from the meat and vegetables. Use long-grain rice, short-grain absorbs too much liquid and becomes stodgy. The lid goes on and is not lifted for 20 minutes.

items.name: Chicken thigh
items.prepNote: boneless, skinless, cut into 3 cm pieces

items.name: Pork sausages
items.prepNote: smoked sausage or chorizo, sliced 1 cm

content.text: Brown the meat and cook the trinity

Heat a little oil in the saucepan over high heat. Season the {{chicken-thigh}} of chicken pieces with salt and pepper and sear for 3 to 4 minutes until browned. Remove. Add the {{sausages-pork}} of sausage slices and brown for 2 minutes per side. Remove. Reduce the heat to medium and add the {{onion}}, {{celery}}, and {{pepper-green}} of the holy trinity. Cook for 8 to 10 minutes until softened and beginning to colour. Add the {{garlic}} of crushed garlic and all the seasoning ({{paprika-smoked}} of paprika, {{oregano-dried}} of oregano, {{thyme}} of thyme, and {{cayenne}} of cayenne) and stir for 1 minute.

Return the chicken and sausage to the pan. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes and {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock. Bring to a simmer. Stir in the {{long-grain-rice}} of rice and distribute evenly. Bring back to a simmer, then cover with a tight lid and cook on a low heat for 20 minutes without lifting the lid. After 20 minutes, remove the lid, the rice should have absorbed all the liquid. If not, replace the lid for a further 5 minutes. Season with salt and pepper and rest for 5 minutes before serving.

items.fix: Replace the lid and cook for a further 5 minutes; if still undercooked, add 100 ml of water and return, covered, for another 5 minutes
items.cause: Lid not sealed properly; too much steam escaped
items.symptom: Rice is still crunchy after 20 minutes

items.fix: Use the stated liquid quantity, the ratio of stock to rice is designed for the absorption method. Remove the lid as soon as the rice is cooked to stop the steam cooking it further
items.cause: Too much liquid, or cooked too long

items.fix: Cook the trinity for the full 8 to 10 minutes until properly softened; the spices should cook with the aromatics for 1 minute before any liquid goes in
items.cause: Holy trinity not cooked long enough, or spices not toasted in the oil

items.fix: The chicken only needs to colour during searing. It will finish cooking in the rice; sear for 3 to 4 minutes and no more
items.cause: Overcooked during the searing phase, or boneless thighs used and cooked too long

content.text: Jambalaya is one of the great dishes of Louisiana cooking. Louisiana cooking is itself one of the most layered regional cuisines in America. It pulls from French colonial rule, Spanish rule, West African food traditions brought by enslaved people, and the Acadian settlers who became the Cajun people. The dish is both a practical one-pot meal and a cultural document. The tomato-based Creole version comes from New Orleans. The non-tomato Cajun version comes from the rural parishes. The two are distinct lineages. Jambalaya is served at festivals, family gatherings, and community events across Louisiana as a sign of regional identity as much as a recipe.
